original_favourites: William Gibson — The Sprawl Trilogy and most other Gibson
Paolo Bacigalupi — all books, especially The Water Knife and The Windup Girl
George R. R. Martin — A Game of Thrones
Joe Abercrombie — all books, especially The First Law
Cixin Liu — The Three-Body Problem and the rest of the trilogy
Bernard Cornwell — especially the Saxon Stories (Uhtred and Alfred the Great)
Ursula K. Le Guin — sci-fi and fantasy
Scott Lynch — Gentleman Bastard / Locke Lamora

genres.label: Climate dystopia
genres.sub: For when you want Bacigalupi, Atwood or McCarthy

genres.label: Space opera and hard sci-fi
genres.sub: For when you want Hyperion, Three Body or Dune

genres.label: Epic fantasy
genres.sub: For when you want Abercrombie, GRRM or Lynch

genres.label: Historical fiction
genres.sub: For when you want Cornwell or Harris

genres.label: Literary speculative
genres.sub: For when you want Le Guin or Mitchell

seed_books.title: Parable of the Sower
seed_books.author: Octavia E. Butler
seed_books.why: Walled communities, climate collapse, a teenage prophet on the road. The bone-deep dread of The Road meets the political thrust of Bacigalupi — and it was written in 1993.

seed_books.title: The Ministry for the Future
seed_books.author: Kim Stanley Robinson
seed_books.why: A polyphonic climate novel that opens with a heatwave scene as devastating as anything in The Water Knife, then pivots into oddly hopeful policy wonkery.

seed_books.title: American War
seed_books.author: Omar El Akkad
seed_books.why: A second US civil war in the 2070s, fought over the last fossil fuels. Spare, devastating prose — distinctly McCarthy-coded, with a slow-burn protagonist arc.

seed_books.title: Gold Fame Citrus
seed_books.author: Claire Vaye Watkins
seed_books.why: California's water is gone and a dune sea is swallowing the Southwest. Lyrical and hallucinatory — basically a literary sibling to The Water Knife.

seed_books.title: Snow Crash
seed_books.author: Neal Stephenson
seed_books.why: Cyberpunk's other foundational text — funnier and faster than Gibson. Samurai pizza couriers, a metaverse linguistic virus, and the best opening 50 pages in the genre.

seed_books.title: Altered Carbon
seed_books.author: Richard Morgan
seed_books.why: Hard-boiled detective noir plus downloadable consciousness. The Sprawl trilogy's 2000s grandchild — grittier, pulpier, and very confident.

seed_books.title: The Quantum Thief
seed_books.author: Hannu Rajaniemi
seed_books.why: A post-cyberpunk caper across a transhuman solar system. Dense, dazzling, throws you in the deep end exactly the way Neuromancer did.

seed_books.title: Blindsight
seed_books.author: Peter Watts
seed_books.why: First-contact horror with transhumans and predator-vampires interrogating consciousness itself. Brutally smart — Hyperion fans tend to love it too.

seed_books.title: A Memory Called Empire
seed_books.author: Arkady Martine
seed_books.why: A small-station ambassador navigates the seductive horror of a vast empire. Reads like Hyperion crossed with Le Guin's anthropological eye. The sequel is just as good.

seed_books.title: The Player of Games
seed_books.author: Iain M. Banks
seed_books.why: The friendliest doorway into the Culture. Scope, civilisational politics, and moral weight that should hit your Dune and Hyperion nerve. Banks is the missing author from your list.

seed_books.title: Children of Time
seed_books.author: Adrian Tchaikovsky
seed_books.why: Uplifted spiders evolve over millennia while a battered generation ship limps toward them. Big-idea SF in Cixin Liu's lineage but with more empathy.

seed_books.title: House of Suns
seed_books.author: Alastair Reynolds
seed_books.why: A six-million-year galactic timescale, with clones of a single woman trading memories at meet-ups every 200,000 years. Hyperion-grade scope and melancholy.

seed_books.title: Gardens of the Moon (Malazan)
seed_books.author: Steven Erikson
seed_books.why: An army-scale, mythologically dense fantasy spread across continents and millennia. Steeper learning curve than First Law but the payoff over ten books is enormous.

seed_books.title: The Black Company
seed_books.author: Glen Cook
seed_books.why: Grimdark before grimdark was a word. Mercenary-company POV, blood-and-mud morality — the obvious ancestor of Abercrombie's First Law.

seed_books.title: Assassin's Apprentice
seed_books.author: Robin Hobb
seed_books.why: Slow-burn, ferocious character work across a 16-book braided saga. The author Rothfuss readers most often graduate to — and she actually finishes her series.

seed_books.title: The Poppy War
seed_books.author: R. F. Kuang
seed_books.why: Military academy to genocide-scale war, drawn from 20th-century Chinese history. Brutal, modern, GRRM-territory stakes — not for the faint-hearted.

seed_books.title: Wolf Hall
seed_books.author: Hilary Mantel
seed_books.why: Thomas Cromwell's rise in Henry VIII's court. Prose good enough to be illegal, plus all the political cunning of GRRM but in actual history. The trilogy is one of the great achievements.

seed_books.title: Shōgun
seed_books.author: James Clavell
seed_books.why: An Englishman shipwrecked into Sengoku-era Japan. Massive, addictive, written with the same propulsive instinct as Cornwell's Saxon books.

seed_books.title: Master and Commander
seed_books.author: Patrick O'Brian
seed_books.why: Napoleonic naval fiction across 20 books. The more literary cousin of Cornwell's Sharpe — quieter, funnier, profoundly companionable once you tune in to the rhythm.

seed_books.title: HHhH
seed_books.author: Laurent Binet
seed_books.why: The 1942 plot to assassinate Reinhard Heydrich, told as a meta-narrative about the difficulty of telling it. Slim, riveting — Robert Harris would adore this one.

seed_books.title: Piranesi
seed_books.author: Susanna Clarke
seed_books.why: A man lives alone in an infinite labyrinth of statues and tides. Short, strange, perfect — Le Guin would have loved it.

seed_books.title: Never Let Me Go
seed_books.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
seed_books.why: Quiet dystopia disguised as a boarding-school memoir. Devastating in the same way Cloud Atlas is devastating, but more concentrated.

seed_books.title: Jonathan Strange & Mr Norrell
seed_books.author: Susanna Clarke
seed_books.why: Two magicians revive English magic during the Napoleonic Wars. Footnoted, funny, immense — the texture of Le Guin with the wit of an Austen novel.

seed_books.title: The Memory Police
seed_books.author: Yoko Ogawa
seed_books.why: On a small island, things vanish from memory one by one — birds, ribbons, novels, body parts. Ishiguro-quiet, Atwood-political, hauntingly beautiful.
